stimulus: Many cultural histories of the Chicano Movement of the 1960s and 1970s rest heavily on manifestos and other explicitly programmatic texts as primary sources, a tendency that can overrepresent the perspectives of a small number of public organizers, most of them men. Historian Yolanda Estrada-Martín has shown that broadening the range of primary sources to include other kinds of material — including mural sketches, family snapshots, and community newspapers — yields a much fuller account of the movement and of the central and varied roles that Chicana women played in shaping it.
stem: Which choice best describes the main idea of the text?

answerOptions.content: Before Estrada-Martín’s work, historians had largely ignored the cultural dimensions of the Chicano Movement.
Estrada-Martín’s methods and research have deepened the historical understanding of the Chicano Movement and Chicana women’s contributions to it.
Other historians of the Chicano Movement have criticized Estrada-Martín’s use of unconventional primary sources.
The Chicano Movement figures whom historians most often cite would have endorsed Estrada-Martín’s conclusions about Chicana women’s roles.

rationale:
Choice B is the best answer. The text begins by saying that prior histories rest too heavily on manifestos, then describes how Estrada-Martín has shown that broader source material yields a fuller account of the movement and of Chicana women’s roles — deepening historical understanding.

Choice A is incorrect because the text says historians have relied on manifestos and programmatic texts, which are themselves cultural in nature. Choice C is incorrect because the text never describes other historians’ reactions to Estrada-Martín’s work. Choice D is incorrect because the text never describes what movement figures would have thought of her conclusions.
